Sentence Level Recurrent Topic Model: Letting Topics Speak for Themselves Fei Tian ∗ University of Science and Technology of China tianfei@mail.ustc.edu.cn Bin Gao Microsoft Research bingao@microsoft.com

Di He

Microsoft Research dihe@microsoft.

c7ebd066-2655-4e82-915a-87103d2f4bee.label: text_chunk
c7ebd066-2655-4e82-915a-87103d2f4bee.text:
com

Tie-Yan Liu Microsoft Research tyliu@microsoft.com

Abstract

We propose Sentence Level Recurrent Topic Model (SLRTM), a new topic model that assumes the gen- eration of each word within a sentence to depend on both the topic of the sentence and the whole history of its preceding words in the sentence.

98e84899-290c-4a91-bd40-5a4b124739a6.label: text_chunk
98e84899-290c-4a91-bd40-5a4b124739a6.text:
Different from conventional topic models that largely ignore the sequential order of words or their topic coher- ence, SLRTM gives full characterization to them by using a Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) based framework.

 Experimental results have shown that SLRTM outperforms several strong baselines on various tasks.

d58cb375-17e6-4e0b-a30a-a776bfcb158a.label: text_chunk
d58cb375-17e6-4e0b-a30a-a776bfcb158a.text:
Furthermore, SLRTM can automat- ically generate sentences given a topic (i.e., topics to sentences), which is a key technology for real world applications such as personalized short text conversation.

1 Introduction

Statistic topic models such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and its variants [Landauer et al. , 1998; Hofmann, 1999; Blei et al.

74658e61-cd38-4014-96fa-74dbc05656c4.label: text_chunk
74658e61-cd38-4014-96fa-74dbc05656c4.text:
, 2003; Mcauliffe and Blei, 2008; Hoff- man et al. , 2010] have been proven to be effective in mod- eling textual documents.

 In these models, a word token in a document is assumed to be generated by a hidden mix- ture model, where the hidden variables are the topic indexes for each word and the topic assignments for words are re- lated to document level topic weights.

97fc282e-1fe1-4868-a891-65812dcc30e2.label: text_chunk
97fc282e-1fe1-4868-a891-65812dcc30e2.text:
Due to the effec- tiveness and efficiency in modeling the document genera- tion process, topic models are widely adopted in quite a lot of real world tasks such as sentiment classification [Mei et al.

 , 2007], social network analysis [Ramage et al. , 2010; Mei et al. , 2007], and recommendation systems [Godin et al.

7f9acb99-3b1b-4913-b24e-cfd8c714bf8a.label: text_chunk
7f9acb99-3b1b-4913-b24e-cfd8c714bf8a.text:
, 2013].Most topic models take the bag-of-words assumption, in which every document is treated as an unordered set of words and the word tokens in such a document are sampled indepen- dently with each other.

 The bag-of-words assumption brings computational convenience, however, it sacrifices the char- acterization of sequential properties of words in a document
and the topic coherence between words belonging to the same language segment (e.g., sentence).

349d0a76-2b3e-4ec6-a8ff-00e5201c354e.label: text_chunk
349d0a76-2b3e-4ec6-a8ff-00e5201c354e.text:
As a result, people have observed many negative examples.

 Just list one for illustra- tion [Wallach, 2006]: the department chair couches offers and the chair department offers couches have very different topics, although they have exactly the same bag of words.There have been some works trying to solve the afore- mentioned problems, although still insufficiently. For exam- ple, several sentence level topic models [Gruber et al.

b03f3e4c-9487-45ae-81c1-af22cf57180e.label: text_chunk
b03f3e4c-9487-45ae-81c1-af22cf57180e.text:
, 2007; Du et al. , 2010; Wang et al.

 , 2011] tackle the topic coher- ence problem by assuming all the words in a sentence to share the same topic (i.e., every sentence has only one topic). In addition, they model the sequential information by as- suming the transition between sentence topics to be Marko- vian.

85c2d1c2-d659-481e-8eee-fb69925d5771.label: text_chunk
85c2d1c2-d659-481e-8eee-fb69925d5771.text:
However, words within the same sentence are still ex- changeable in these models, and thus the bag-of-words as- sumption still holds within a sentence.

 For another exam- ple, in [Yang et al. , 2015], the embedding based neural lan- guage model [Bengio et al. , 2003; Mikolov et al.

fd85a9bb-9881-49ee-aaa5-5c656540305f.label: text_chunk
fd85a9bb-9881-49ee-aaa5-5c656540305f.text:
, 2013; Le and Mikolov, 2014] and topic model are integrated.

 They assume the generation of a given word in a sentence to depend on its local context (including its preceding words within a fixed window) as well as the topics of the sentence and doc- ument it lies in. However, using a fixed window of preceding words, instead of the whole word stream within a sentence, could only introduce limited sequential dependency.

3718cccd-3488-4e68-9ede-14b890ec2c5a.label: text_chunk
3718cccd-3488-4e68-9ede-14b890ec2c5a.text: Further- more, there is no explicit coherence constraints on the word topics and sentence topics, since every word can have its own topics in their model.We propose Sentence Level Recurrent Topic Model (SLRTM) to tackle the limitations of the aforementioned works.

44b36719-d99c-4966-a03a-0e7d104c4e3a.label: text_chunk
44b36719-d99c-4966-a03a-0e7d104c4e3a.text: In the new model, we assume the words in the same sentence to share the same topic in order to guarantee topic coherence, and we assume the generation of a word to rely on the whole history in the same sentence in order to fully characterize the sequential dependency.

4e0aafb6-c417-430c-848b-34ce2cd6579e.label: text_chunk
4e0aafb6-c417-430c-848b-34ce2cd6579e.text: Specifically, for a particular word w within a sentence s , we assume its gen- eration depends on two factors: the first is the whole set of its historical words in the sentence and the second is the sen- tence topic, which we regard as a pseudo word and has its own distributed representations. We use Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) [Mikolov et al.

d48e3cd7-3039-4344-9e39-886adbfebca7.label: text_chunk
d48e3cd7-3039-4344-9e39-886adbfebca7.text:
, 2010], such as Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) [Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997] or Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) network [Cho et al. , 2014],
∗ This work was done when the two authors were visiting Mi- crosoft Research Asia.to model such a long term dependency.

3f06ffa6-823c-464f-8b52-c58c71d2e923.label: text_chunk
3f06ffa6-823c-464f-8b52-c58c71d2e923.text: With the proposed SLRTM, we can not only model the doc- ument generation process more accurately, but also construct new natural sentences that are coherent with a given topic (we call it topic2sentence , similar to image2sentece [Vinyals et al. , 2015]). Topic2sentence has its huge potential for many real world tasks.

cda0b3d3-d702-405a-a382-ad2b58bb3f87.label: text_chunk
cda0b3d3-d702-405a-a382-ad2b58bb3f87.text:
For example, it can serve as the basis of per- sonalized short text conversation system [Shang et al.

 , 2015; Serban et al.

691701ae-b90a-438f-a7dc-2397b76b8271.label: text_chunk
691701ae-b90a-438f-a7dc-2397b76b8271.text:
, 2015], in which once we detect that the user is interested in certain topics, we can let these topics speak for themselves using SLRTM to improve the user satisfactory.We have conducted experiments to compare SLRTM with several strong topic model baselines on two tasks: genera- tive model evaluation (i.e. test set perplexity) and document classification.

 The results on several benchmark datasets quantitatively demonstrate SLRTM’s advantages in modeling documents.

eccc7ee3-9fba-41c8-9721-6e25ad0b2ed5.label: text_chunk
eccc7ee3-9fba-41c8-9721-6e25ad0b2ed5.text:
We further provide some qualitative results on topic2sentence , the generated sentences for different topics clearly demonstrate the power of SLRTM in topic-sensitive short text conversations.

2 Related Work

One of the most representative topic models is Latent Dirich- let Allocation [Blei et al. , 2003], in which every word in a document has its topic drawn from document level topic weights.

bcc6f9ff-e769-4edb-b178-0c9b78c63d20.label: text_chunk
bcc6f9ff-e769-4edb-b178-0c9b78c63d20.text:
Several variants of LDA have been developed such as hierarchical topic models [Blei et al. , 2004] and supervised topic models [Mcauliffe and Blei, 2008].

 With the recent development of deep learning, there are also neural network based topic models such as [Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2009; Larochelle and Lauly, 2012; Cao et al.

8a5dc30a-1f0b-445d-8435-8cf6f506c1bf.label: text_chunk
8a5dc30a-1f0b-445d-8435-8cf6f506c1bf.text:
, 2015; Das et al. , 2015], which use distributed representations of words to im- prove topic semantics.

Most of the aforementioned works take the bag-of-words assumption, which might be too simple according to our dis- cussions in the introduction. That is, it ignores both sequen- tial dependency of words and topic coherence of words.There are some efforts trying to address the limitations of the bag-of-words assumption.

10e17d79-6b54-467e-9cd5-2d130b714456.label: text_chunk
10e17d79-6b54-467e-9cd5-2d130b714456.text:
For example, in [Griffiths et al. , 2004], both semantic (i.e.

, related with topics) and syn- tactic properties of words were modeled. After that, a hidden Markov transition model for topics was proposed [Gruber et al. , 2007], in which all the words in a sentence were regarded as having the same topic.

b6bfc128-cd69-4b71-9af6-cd1901857bcf.label: text_chunk
b6bfc128-cd69-4b71-9af6-cd1901857bcf.text:
Such a one sentence, one topic assumption was also used by some other works, including [Du et al. , 2010; Wang et al. , 2011].

 Although these works have made some meaningful attempts on topic coherence and sequential dependency across sentences, they have not suf- ficiently model the sequential dependency of words within a sentence. To address this problem, the authors of [Yang et al.

549a8711-666e-498b-ab1b-ccad8bf16416.label: text_chunk
549a8711-666e-498b-ab1b-ccad8bf16416.text:
, 2015] adopted the neural language model technology [Bengio et al. , 2003] to enhance topic model.

 In particular, they assume that every document, sentence, and word have their own topics and the topical information is conveyed by their embedding vectors through a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) as a prior.

c8ec3a34-b368-4c11-a076-e165b9acb93b.label: text_chunk
c8ec3a34-b368-4c11-a076-e165b9acb93b.text:
In the GMM distribution, each topic cor- responds to a mixture parameterized by the mean vector and covariance matrix of the Gaussian distribution. The embed-
ding vectors sampled from the GMM are further used to gen- erate words in a sentence according to a feedforward neural network.

1cb88599-1721-4736-9b77-03351f80d276.label: text_chunk
1cb88599-1721-4736-9b77-03351f80d276.text: To be specific, the preceding words in a fixed sized window, together with the sentence and document, act as the context to generate the next word by a softmax conditional distribution, in which the context is represented by embed- ding vectors. While this work has explicitly modeled the se- quential dependency of words, it ignores the topic coherence among adjacent words.

ce2d109a-d7a3-4396-b6ec-cf2f1520e0c0.label: text_chunk
ce2d109a-d7a3-4396-b6ec-cf2f1520e0c0.text: Another line of research related to our model is Recur- rent Neural Network (RNN), especially some recently devel- oped effective RNN models such as Long Short Term Mem- ory [Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997] and Gated Recurrent Unit [Cho et al. , 2014].

cbd3f4ff-474f-4faa-a079-a802d60fd1cf.label: text_chunk
cbd3f4ff-474f-4faa-a079-a802d60fd1cf.text: These new RNN models characterize long range dependencies for a sequence, and has been widely adopted in sequence modeling tasks such as machine trans- lation [Cho et al. , 2014] and short text conversation [Shang et al. , 2015].

d9d5404e-52c5-48ca-a656-77ec25733420.label: text_chunk
d9d5404e-52c5-48ca-a656-77ec25733420.text:
In particular, for language modeling tasks, it has been shown that RNN (and its variants such as LSTM) is much more effective than simple feedforward neural net- works with fixed window size [Mikolov et al. , 2010] given that it can model dependencies with nearly arbitrary length.

3 Sentence Level Recurrent Topic Model

In this section, we describe the proposed Sentence Level Re- current Topic Model (SLRTM).

e16f1ca8-b7de-45e8-ac0c-f79e23107e7d.label: text_chunk
e16f1ca8-b7de-45e8-ac0c-f79e23107e7d.text:
First of all, we list three im- portant design factors in SLRTM as below.

• SLRTM takes the one sentence,

 one topic assumption as in [Gruber et al. , 2007; Du et al. , 2010; Wang et al. , 2011]: all words within the same sentence share the same topic.

74fe1a25-03ca-45fb-a9ef-83ec6481b7f0.label: text_chunk
74fe1a25-03ca-45fb-a9ef-83ec6481b7f0.text:
This assumption guarantees the topic coher- ence within a sentence, and makes topic2sentence pos- sible.

• To model long range dependencies between words,

 SLRTM uses RNN (specifically LSTM) with word em- bedding vectors as input.

0fe0f44d-7c0c-48c9-b840-f6efdb91618a.label: text_chunk
0fe0f44d-7c0c-48c9-b840-f6efdb91618a.text: The purpose is to leverage word embeddings to enhance the semantics of words, as indicated by the previous neural network based topic models [Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2009; Larochelle and Lauly, 2012; Cao et al. , 2015; Das et al. , 2015].

ee69a535-40a3-4fbb-8033-8ef82b73ba1d.label: text_chunk
ee69a535-40a3-4fbb-8033-8ef82b73ba1d.text:
• Each topic in SLRTM has its own distributed representa- tion,

 which is fine-tuned through the training process and is used to generate the whole sentence. Topic represen- tation vector plays a similar role to the source sentence representation in LSTM based machine translation [Cho et al. , 2014] and the image vector output by Convolu- tional Neural Network in image captioning [Vinyals et al. , 2015].

e2cf5a6f-55f0-4be6-84f8-6af57e0234a7.label: text_chunk
e2cf5a6f-55f0-4be6-84f8-6af57e0234a7.text:
With the three points in mind, let us introduce the detailed generative process of SLRTM, as well as the stochastic vari- ational inference and learning algorithm for SLRTM in the following subsections.

3.1 The generative process

Suppose we have K topics, |W| words contained in dictio- nary W , and M documents D = { d 1 , · · · , d M } .

document d i , i ∈ { 1 , 2 , · · · , M } , it is composed of N i sen- tences and its j th sentence s ij consists of T ij words.

 Sim- ilar to LDA, we assume there is a K -dimensional Dirichlet prior distribution Dir ( α ) for topic mixture weights of each document.

4 Experiments

We report our experimental results in this section.

4e93a80e-2a8d-479d-aaa3-13d778a828d2.label: text_chunk
4e93a80e-2a8d-479d-aaa3-13d778a828d2.text: Our experi- ments include two parts: (1) quantitative experiments, includ- ing a generative document evaluation task and a document classification task, on two datasets; (2) qualitative inspection, including the examination of the sentences generated under each topic, in order to test whether SLRTM performs well in the topic2sentence task.

0dec31f7-1380-457f-a5f8-9274b668517f.label: text_chunk
0dec31f7-1380-457f-a5f8-9274b668517f.text:
4.1 Quantitative Results

We compare SLRTM with several state-of-the-art topic mod- els on two tasks: generative document evaluation and docu- ment classification.

3a15f72d-b2d2-4ddb-8e58-23128438795e.label: text_chunk
3a15f72d-b2d2-4ddb-8e58-23128438795e.text:
The former task is to investigate the gen- eration capability of the models, while the latter is to show the representation ability of the models.We base our experiments on two benchmark datasets:

• 20Newsgroup , which contains 18,

845 emails catego- rized into 20 different topical groups such as religion , politics , and sports . The dataset is originally partitioned into 11,314 training documents and 7,531 test docu- ments 2 .

22beb7d3-5190-4243-83c0-b621a5696ffb.label: text_chunk
22beb7d3-5190-4243-83c0-b621a5696ffb.text:
• Wiki10+ [Zubiaga, 2012] 3 ,

 which contains Web docu-
ments from Wikipedia, each of which is associated with several tags such as philosophy , software , and music . Following [Cao et al. , 2015], we kept the most frequent 25 tags and removed those documents without any of these tags, forming a training set and a test set with 11,164 and 6,161 documents, respectively.

25bb1ffd-a7ed-4cee-9675-fe0c1906f6cb.label: text_chunk
25bb1ffd-a7ed-4cee-9675-fe0c1906f6cb.text:
The social tags associated with each document are regarded as su- pervised labels in classification.

 Wiki10+ contains much more words per document (i.e., 1,704) than 20News- group (i.e., 135).We followed the practice in many previous works and re- moved infrequent words. After that, the dictionary con- tains about 32 k unique words for 20Newsgroup and 41 k for Wiki10+.

43363420-23e9-4f9d-bc0e-c8eae67cb73d.label: text_chunk
43363420-23e9-4f9d-bc0e-c8eae67cb73d.text:
We adopted the NLTK sentence tokenizer 4 to split the datasets into sentences if sentence boundaries are needed.The following baselines were used in our experiments:

• LDA [Blei et al. 2003].

 LDA is the classic topic model, and we used GibbsLDA++ 5 for its implementation.

• Doc-NADE [Larochelle and Lauly, 2012].

 Doc-NADE is a representative neural network based topic model.

b3affec1-8ee2-499b-ba39-e68b2453231d.label: text_chunk
b3affec1-8ee2-499b-ba39-e68b2453231d.text:
We used the open-source code provided by the authors.

• HTMM [Gruber et al. 2007].

 HTMM models consider the sentence level Markov transitions. Similar to Doc- NADE, the implementation was provided by the authors.

• GMNTM [Yang et al. 2015].

 GMNTM considers mod- els the order of words within a sentence by a feedforward neural network.

686aecdc-179d-45f8-a030-af81651bbacd.label: text_chunk
686aecdc-179d-45f8-a030-af81651bbacd.text:
We implemented GMNTM according the descriptions in their papers by our own.

Experimental Setting

For SLRTM, we implemented it in C++ using Eigen 6 and Intel MKL. For the sake of fairness, similar to [Yang et al. , 2015], we set the word embedding size, topic embed- ding size, and LSTM hidden layer size to be 128 , 128 , and 600 respectively.

Conclusion

In this paper, we proposed a novel topic model called Sen- tence Level Recurrent Topic Model (SLRTM), which mod- els the sequential dependency of words and topic coher- ence within a sentence using Recurrent Neural Networks, and shows superior performance in both predictive document modeling and document classification. In addition, it makes topic2sentence possible, which can benefit many real world tasks such as personalized short text conversation (STC).

62ad16b8-a578-4ce9-99b5-376311da2b38.label: text_chunk
62ad16b8-a578-4ce9-99b5-376311da2b38.text:
In the future, we plan to integrate SLRTM into RNN-based STC systems [Shang et al. , 2015] to make the dialogue more topic sensitive. We would also like to conduct large scale SLRTM training on bigger corpus with more topics by spe- cially designed scalable algorithms and computational plat- forms.

The SLRTM (Structured Latent Relational Topic Model) is a sophisticated model designed to enhance the process of generating coherent sentences related to specific topics, known as topic2sentence. It aims to improve user satisfaction by effectively modeling topics of interest to users. The model is built upon the foundational work of Du et al., which discusses the one sentence, one topic assumption, highlighting its relevance in the context of SLRTM.

SLRTM is capable of analyzing words from a dictionary (W) to extract topics from documents and is implemented in C++, showcasing its technical foundation. It has been rigorously tested against baseline models, consistently outperforming them, which underscores its superior capability in modeling document generation processes. The model's performance is evaluated using perplexity, a metric that assesses its effectiveness.

In comparative studies, SLRTM has been shown to outperform other models, including Doc-NADE, in topic modeling tasks. Furthermore, there are plans to integrate SLRTM into RNN-based STC (Sequence-to-Sequence Topic Classification) systems, which would enhance their sensitivity to topics in dialogues. Additionally, SLRTM has a connection to the geographical feature of Crater River, indicating its potential application in analyzing or generating topics related to this area.
